itineraries.title: Gaspésie Loop: From Rimouski to the End of the World
itineraries.subtitle: A 1,400-km loop between cliffs, fjords, and fishing villages, from the Bas-Saint-Laurent to the tip of Forillon and back via the Baie des Chaleurs.

visits.text: The Pointe-au-Père Maritime Historic Site unfolds around three focal points. The Empress of Ireland Museum chronicles the sinking of May 29, 1914: in just 14 minutes, the Canadian Pacific liner went down after being rammed by the Norwegian collier Storstad in thick fog, taking 1,012 of the 1,477 people aboard with her. This remains Canada's worst maritime disaster, though it was quickly overshadowed by the outbreak of World War I two months later. The wreck still rests at 45 meters depth off the coast of Sainte-Luce.

The HMCS Onondaga submarine, an Oberon-class vessel, stretches 90 meters long. Launched in 1965 and decommissioned in 2000, it's the only submarine in Canada open to visitors. You descend through narrow hatches into the vessel's belly to discover the torpedo room, command post, and stacked bunks where 70 submariners slept.

The Pointe-au-Père Lighthouse, built in 1909, is an octagonal reinforced concrete tower standing 33 meters tall—one of Canada's tallest lighthouses. It guided navigation through the St. Lawrence estuary for nearly a century. Climb the stairs for panoramic views of the river, which spans 40 km wide at this point.

The Musée régional de Rimouski occupies eastern Quebec's oldest stone church, built in 1824. Exhibitions alternate between contemporary art, science, and regional history of the Bas-Saint-Laurent. The building itself, with its neoclassical façade, is worth the visit.

Maison Lamontagne, built in 1744, is designated a national heritage building. It's one of the rare surviving examples of New France architecture still standing in the region. Its construction blends colombage pierroté (half-timbering with stone infill) and pièce sur pièce (horizontal log construction), both typical techniques of the colonial era.

activities.text: Stroll along the Promenade de la Mer at sunset. Rimouski was named Quebec's 'City of Happiness' in 2013, and its estuary sunsets rank among the province's most spectacular.

Cross by kayak or zodiac to Île Saint-Barnabé, a 4-km-long island facing Rimouski. A hermit named Toussaint Cartier lived there alone from 1728 to 1767. Hiking trails traverse the island through forest and shoreline.

practical_info.tip: Book the crossing in advance during high season

visits.text: Bic National Park, established in 1984, covers 33.2 km² between Route 132 and the St. Lawrence estuary. This territory has been inhabited for over 8,000 years, as evidenced by some thirty Indigenous archaeological sites. Samuel de Champlain anchored here in 1603 and gave his name to the peak dominating the park. The landscape consists of rock ridges folded by the Appalachians, forming an intricate lace of bays, coves, headlands and islands along 30 km of coastline.

Pic Champlain rises to 346 meters elevation—the highest point in the natural region of the estuary's southern shore. The trail climbs through a maple grove before opening onto a panorama embracing the islands, Ha! Ha! Bay, and the 34-km-wide estuary. The park harbors the continent's highest concentration of North American porcupines and harbor seal colonies observable from Anse aux Bouleaux Ouest.

Le Grand Rassemblement in Sainte-Flavie marks the 'gateway to Gaspésie.' In 1986, sculptor Marcel Gagnon began placing life-sized concrete statues in the St. Lawrence waters. Today, about 120 figures emerge from the river with the tides, in a long silent procession toward land. The artwork transforms with light and water level—at low tide, the silhouettes rise in full; at high tide, only heads protrude.

The Reford Gardens in Grand-Métis have been a National Historic Site of Canada since 1995. Elsie Reford, a self-taught horticulturist who didn't take up gardening until age 54 following an appendix operation, transformed between 1926 and 1958 a fishing camp inherited from her uncle George Stephen (founder of Canadian Pacific Railway) into English-style gardens. The 18-hectare estate shelters 3,000 plant species and varieties, including the famous Himalayan blue poppy. The microclimate created by the confluence of the Mitis River and the St. Lawrence allows cultivation of species found nowhere else in Canada. Villa Estevan, built in 1887, is a historic 37-room house.

activities.text: Hike the Grand Tour at Bic Park: this 8.7-km loop follows the rocky shoreline and is only passable at low tide. Start about two hours before the lowest tide to explore the rock formations and observe seals on the rocks.

practical_info.tip: Check tide schedule before departing; sturdy footwear essential

activities.text: Sea kayaking excursion through the Bic archipelago, weaving between islands and headlands. Harbor seal colonies bask on the rocks and common eider ducks nest on the islets. In the background, the setting sun illuminates the cliffs.

practical_info.tip: Guided outings available during summer season

map_keywords: Parc national de la Gaspésie Sainte-Anne-des-Monts

visits.text: Gaspésie National Park, established in 1937, protects 802 km² in the heart of the peninsula. It contains more than 25 peaks exceeding 1,000 meters, including 7 of Quebec's 10 highest. The Chic-Choc Mountains—from the Mi'kmaq word meaning 'impassable barrier'—form a 95-km-long range parallel to the river. Mont Albert, at 1,154 meters, is composed of serpentine, a rock usually buried in the Earth's mantle and rarely exposed at the surface. Its summit plateau harbors alpine tundra with Arctic plants, at a latitude where you'd normally find boreal forest.

Lac aux Américains is a glacial cirque formed about 10,000 years ago, nestled at the foot of steep walls. The 2.4-km trail (round-trip) crosses a boreal forest of balsam firs before opening onto this natural basin of dark waters. The name recalls the Americans who came to fish for Atlantic salmon in the Sainte-Anne River in the 19th century.

The Martre Lighthouse, en route to Sainte-Anne-des-Monts, is one of Quebec's rare wooden lighthouses. Painted red, it was built in 1906 and designated a heritage building. It dominates the Haute-Gaspésie shoreline and houses a small interpretation center on St. Lawrence lighthouses.

activities.text: Ascent of Mont Albert: the 11.6-km loop with 860 meters of elevation gain crosses boreal forest, then alpine tundra. At the summit, the serpentine plateau offers panoramic views of the Chic-Chocs. The park was originally created to protect the Gaspésie caribou herd—estimated at fewer than 50 individuals—the last remaining south of the St. Lawrence.

practical_info.tip: Bring warm clothing; the summit is exposed to wind and temperature can drop 10°C compared to the base

activities.text: For a more accessible option, the Mont Ernest-Laforce trail (5.2 km loop) offers viewpoints over valleys and summits without the difficulty of alpine ascent.

visits.text: Percé Rock is a limestone monolith 433 meters long and 88 meters high, pierced by a natural arch 15 meters wide. The vertical strata composing it belong to the Forillon Formation and date from the Lower Devonian, between 394 and 418 million years ago. They contain fossils of brachiopods and trilobites. Until 1845, the rock had two arches—the second collapsed. Marine erosion drops several tons of rock each year, and access to the base of the rock is prohibited for safety reasons.

The Percé UNESCO Global Geopark offers 18 km of hiking trails through 500-million-year-old geology. The Mont Sainte-Anne trail leads to a natural cave, a spectacular crevasse, and a forest of cedars twisted by wind, nicknamed the 'magic forest.' At the top of the climb, a glass platform suspended at 200 meters altitude overlooks the village and Percé Rock—Gaspésie's most photographed spot. The immersive Tektonik exhibition in the welcome pavilion takes you back through Percé's geological time.

The Promenade de la Grave runs along the village waterfront. Percé takes its name from Basque and Breton fishermen who frequented the bay as early as the 16th century for cod. The village was an important fishing center under the regime of Jersey companies, including Charles Robin & Company, which dominated the dried cod trade for over a century.

activities.text: Hike the Geopark trails: the Mont Sainte-Anne loop (about 4 km) passes through the crevasse, cave, and magic forest before reaching the suspended glass platform.
Sunset walk along La Grave and Cap Mont-Joli for the best views of Percé Rock bathed in golden light.

map_keywords: Colonie fous de Bassan île Bonaventure

visits.text: Bonaventure Island and Percé Rock National Park protects exceptional natural, historical, and geological heritage. Bonaventure Island, covering 4.16 km², harbors the world's most accessible Northern Gannet colony—about 110,000 birds nest each summer on the red conglomerate cliffs of the eastern coast. The Northern Gannet is a seabird with white plumage, an orange-tinted head, and black wings, with a wingspan reaching up to 2 meters. It dives like an arrow at over 100 km/h to catch fish.

The island was inhabited by fishing families until 1971. The historic village, with its houses and vigneaux (wooden structures for drying cod), testifies to a vanished way of life. Le Boutillier House, the former fishing manager's residence, is open to visitors free of charge and displays period furniture.

The Emerald River Falls, in Percé's backcountry, lives up to its name: water cascades into a natural basin of intense turquoise. A short forest trail leads there for a refreshing break away from the coast.

activities.text: Cruise to Bonaventure Island: the boat first circles close to Percé Rock, then runs along the island's cliffs where thousands of birds nest—black-legged kittiwakes, cormorants, razorbills, and Atlantic puffins. Once landed on the island, take the Sentier des Mousses (3.1 km) going and the Chemin du Roy returning for varied landscapes. At the end, the Northern Gannet colony extends just meters from the observation boardwalk.

practical_info.tip: Leave early morning to avoid crowds; bring a picnic as island dining services are limited

activities.text: Whale watching cruise: seven whale species frequent the waters off Percé, including minke whales, fin whales, and occasionally humpback whales.

visits.text: Gaspé is the birthplace of French America. On July 24, 1534, Jacques Cartier planted a 30-foot cross here to claim the territory in the name of King François I—a gesture that prompted protests from Iroquoian chief Donnacona. The Musée de la Gaspésie on the boulevard presents the permanent exhibition 'Le Grand Large,' which tells the peninsula's story through 15 sailing vessels, from Viking drakkars to Mi'kmaq ocean-going canoes. In front of the museum, six bronze steles by artist Gérard Leblanc compose the monument commemorating the encounter of two civilizations.

The Christ-Roi Cathedral in Gaspé, consecrated in 1969, is one of the only cathedrals built entirely of wood in North America. Its red cedar structure and modern stained glass windows make it a singular architectural monument. It replaced a previous church destroyed by fire.

The Gespeg Mi'gmaq Culture Interpretation Site recreates a 17th-century Mi'kmaq village, at the time of the first Europeans' arrival. Birchbark wigwams, fire circles, utilitarian objects, and traditional crafts immerse visitors in this millennia-old culture. The word 'Gespeg' means 'land's end' in Mi'kmaq—it gave its name to the town and the entire peninsula.

The Cap-des-Rosiers Lighthouse, erected in 1858, is Canada's tallest lighthouse at 34 meters. It stands at the entrance to Forillon National Park, on a rocky cape battered by gulf waves. Its name comes from explorer Samuel de Champlain, who noted the abundance of wild roses on this point in 1632.

activities.text: Walk the Jacques-Cartier Promenade along the York River and Gaspé Bay. Period houses and shops line the route, and on some summer evenings, a projection show recounts Cartier's arrival on a giant screen mimicking his ship's sails.
Mackerel fishing from the Gaspé wharf: the activity is open to all, with or without experience, and equipment is easy to find on-site.

visits.text: Forillon National Park, created in 1970, spans 245 km² at the northeastern tip of the Gaspésie Peninsula—where the Appalachians end their 3,200-km journey in the St. Lawrence waters. Its creation forced the expropriation of 225 families whose villages were razed, a painful episode in local history. The park's rocks—limestone, sandstone, and mudstone—date from the Ordovician to the Devonian (488 to 359 million years ago). Ten superimposed geological formations in bands are exposed here, a rare phenomenon.

Cap-Bon-Ami offers one of the park's most striking panoramas: vertical limestone cliffs plunge into turquoise water, with a pebble beach at the base. In spring, the Raoul-Roy lookout allows observation of raptor migration. The sector is also the starting point for Mont Saint-Alban, whose observation tower at 285 meters altitude provides 360-degree views—on clear days, you can glimpse Percé Rock and Bonaventure Island in the distance.

The Grande-Grave Heritage Site, on the park's southern coast, preserves buildings from the cod fishing era. The Hyman & Sons general store, built in the 1860s, functioned as a trading post where fishermen exchanged their catch for goods. Anse-Blanchette recreates the life of a farming-fishing family from the early 20th century. Fort Péninsule, near Penouille, was fortified during World War II to defend Gaspé Bay against German submarines.

The Les Graves trail leads to Cap-Gaspé, where you'll find 'the end of the world': a red and white lighthouse and a lookout perched above the sea. This is where the Gaspésie Peninsula ends in a rocky point plunging into gulf waters. Whales are regularly observed offshore from this point.

activities.text: Hike Mont Saint-Alban: 7.8-km loop starting from Cap-Bon-Ami. The climb is steep for the first kilometer, then the trail crosses boreal forest before reaching the observation tower at 285 meters.
Hike to Cap-Gaspé via the Les Graves trail: 8-km round trip from Anse-aux-Amérindiens, following the Gaspé Bay coast between forest and shoreline.
Sunset sea kayaking in Gaspé Bay, departing from Cap-aux-Os beach. The 3-hour guided outings allow observation of grey seals, seabirds, and the sun descending behind Forillon's cliffs.

to_next_leg.road_type: Route 132 South along the Baie des Chaleurs

visits.text: The route between Gaspé and Carleton-sur-Mer follows the Baie des Chaleurs, named by Jacques Cartier in July 1534 because of the summer heat prevailing there. This is where he made the first documented trade exchanges between the French and Mi'kmaq—furs for European objects. This arm of the Gulf of St. Lawrence benefits from a microclimate that makes the water noticeably warmer than the rest of the estuary.

Stop at Bonaventure en route: the Bioparc de la Gaspésie presents the peninsula's five ecosystems—the bay, river, boreal forest, tundra, and shoreline—with indigenous fauna in semi-liberty (black bears, lynx, caribou, otters). The Musée acadien du Québec, on the same street, traces the history of Acadians deported during the Great Upheaval of 1755, some of whom fled to Gaspésie with Mi'kmaq help.

Mont Saint-Joseph rises to 555 meters elevation, just 5 km from the shore. A paved road climbs to the summit, but the hiking trails (30 km total) are more rewarding. At the summit, the view plunges over the Baie des Chaleurs, Carleton's triangular barachois, the Gaspésian backcountry, and the New Brunswick coasts across the bay. Notre-Dame Oratory, a Breton-style chapel built in 1935 by the local community, is notable for its interior mosaics and colored glass windows.

The Carleton-sur-Mer barachois is a brackish body of water separated from the sea by a sand spit—Pointe Tracadigache. This type of coastal lagoon shelters rich biodiversity of birds and fish. The 6-km bike path that loops around it offers a unique perspective on the junction between sea and mountain. As early as the mid-19th century, city dwellers and pilgrims came to enjoy this landscape, making Carleton one of Gaspésie's oldest resort destinations.

activities.text: Hike Mont Saint-Joseph: the Taguine trail (5 km) followed by the Cap-Ferré extension (3.6 km) offers an 8.6-km round trip to the summit with progressive views over the Baie des Chaleurs.
Bike tour around the barachois (6 km) late in the day, with a stop at Pointe Tracadigache for sunset and, on some summer evenings, open-air storytelling.

visits.text: Miguasha National Park, inscribed as a UNESCO World Heritage Site in 1999, protects a 380-million-year-old fossil site. In the Mi'kmaq language, 'Miguasha' means 'red land,' referring to the color of the cliffs. The Escuminac Formation, 120 meters thick, contains 21 fossil fish species representing 5 of the 6 major groups that lived in the Devonian—only sharks and rays are missing. The site has yielded more than 18,000 specimens since its discovery in 1842 by geologist Abraham Gesner.

Eusthenopteron foordi, nicknamed the 'Prince of Miguasha,' was a fish equipped with lungs and bony structures in its fins, illustrating the transition from aquatic to terrestrial life. But the museum's current star is Elpistostege watsoni—'Elpi' for short—a 1.6-meter fossil discovered in 2010, unique in the world, considered the closest relative of the first tetrapods. It's a key piece for understanding how vertebrates conquered dry land.

The town of Bonaventure has the world's longest aluminum pedestrian walkway. The Circuit des Bâtisseurs, a bike path lined with illuminated interpretation panels at night, tells the story of the last century in the Baie des Chaleurs.

activities.text: Visit the Miguasha natural history museum and descend to the fossil cliff with a naturalist guide. The 'Through the Geologist's Eye' activity allows you to observe sedimentary layers in situ and understand how fossils form. The 'Evolution of Life' trail (3.5 km) then offers a wooded hike with views over the Restigouche estuary.

Kayak or canoe descent on the Bonaventure River, renowned for its emerald-colored transparent waters. The river, 125 km long, flows through forests and red sandstone cliffs. Guided descents last from 2 to 7 hours depending on the chosen route.

practical_info.tip: Book in advance; spots fill quickly in high season

map_keywords: Canyon des Portes de l'Enfer Rimouski

visits.text: The Matapédia Valley, taken on the return route between Carleton and Rimouski, is a glacial valley carved between the Notre-Dame Mountains. The Matapédia River, 73 km long, is one of Quebec's most renowned for Atlantic salmon fishing. Route 132 then Route 6 wind between dense forests and forestry villages.

The Canyon des Portes de l'Enfer in Saint-Narcisse-de-Rimouski takes its name from the deep gorges carved by the Rimouski River. The suspension bridge spanning the canyon peaks at 63 meters height—Quebec's highest suspension bridge. A 300-step staircase, baptized 'la descente aux enfers' (the descent into hell), reaches the river level below. The trails also pass by the Grand Sault waterfall and a tribute to the log drivers, those men who floated timber down the river in the forestry era.

activities.text: Hike at Canyon des Portes de l'Enfer: several hiking trails offer 2 to 12 km of routes between lookouts, bridges, and waterfalls. Crossing the 63-meter suspension bridge is the highlight.

practical_info.tip: Not for those with fear of heights on the bridge

activities.text: Sunset over the river from the Rimouski promenade, to close this Gaspésie loop.

seo.h1_title: Gaspésie Road Trip: Where the Appalachians Meet the Sea
seo.intro_paragraph: The Gaspésie Peninsula stretches like a wild finger into the Gulf of St. Lawrence, where 500-million-year-old geology meets caribou-dotted alpine tundra and 110,000 Northern Gannets dive into turquoise waters. This 1,400-km loop from Rimouski through Percé and Forillon reveals a land shaped by ice, time, and the Atlantic—from the wreck of the Empress of Ireland to the fossil cliffs of UNESCO-listed Miguasha, where life first learned to walk on land.
seo.seo_hook: Gaspésie is a 30,000-km² peninsula where the Appalachians plunge into the St. Lawrence, where 110,000 Northern Gannets nest facing Percé Rock, and where a 380-million-year-old fossil tells the story of when life left the water to conquer land.
featured_list: Rimouski: Empress of Ireland wreck site and HMCS Onondaga submarine
Bic Park: harbor seals, kayaking, and coastal trails on the estuary
Percé: the Rock, the Geopark, and Bonaventure Island
Forillon: peninsula's end, cliffs, and whales
Carleton-sur-Mer: Mont Saint-Joseph and the barachois
Miguasha: Devonian fossils, UNESCO World Heritage

places_taglines.CA::rimouski: Where the St. Lawrence widens into the gulf and history rests beneath the waves
places_taglines.CA::le-bic: A glacial archipelago where seals bask on rocks at low tide
places_taglines.CA::sainte-anne-des-monts: Gateway to the Chic-Chocs and Canada's last caribou herd south of the St. Lawrence
places_taglines.CA::perce: Where 500 million years of geology towers above the sea and 110,000 gannets dive for fish
places_taglines.CA::gaspe: Birthplace of French America, where Cartier planted his cross in 1534
places_taglines.CA::carleton-sur-mer: An Acadian village cradled between mountain summit and turquoise bay

highlights: Percé Rock and the 110,000-strong Northern Gannet colony on Bonaventure Island
Forillon National Park, where the Appalachians plunge into the St. Lawrence
The Miguasha fossil site, UNESCO World Heritage
Gaspésie National Park and the Chic-Choc peaks above 1,000 meters
Bic National Park and its harbor seals on the maritime estuary
The Reford Gardens, a National Historic Site created by Elsie Reford starting in 1926

itineraries.title: Saguenay Fjord & Lac-Saint-Jean Loop
itineraries.subtitle: From Jacques-Cartier Park to the Saguenay Fjord's towering cliffs, then around Lac Saint-Jean—1,200 km looping from Québec City through boreal forest, fjord villages, and the Lake's rich terroir
itineraries.notes: Loop departing from and returning to Québec City. Route 175 crosses the Laurentides Wildlife Reserve for 200 km with no villages or gas stations—fill up before departure. Two return options offered on day 9: via Mauricie or via Tadoussac. 100% nature and regional terroir circuit.
specialties: Artisan cheeses from the Saguenay region

map_keywords: Parc national de la Jacques-Cartier Québec

visits.text: Route 175 North from Québec City: after 30 minutes, you enter the Laurentides Wildlife Reserve, a vast boreal forest stretching 200 km between Québec and the Saguenay. No villages, very few gas stations along this stretch—fill up before you leave. The four-lane highway cuts through a landscape of lakes, black spruce, and rounded hills sculpted by glaciers.

Jacques-Cartier National Park (670 km², established 1981): just 30 minutes from Québec City, the park protects one of Québec's most dramatic glacial valleys. The Jacques-Cartier Valley drops more than 550 meters—the walls plunge from a high mountain plateau down to a meandering river at the bottom. Up top, boreal conifer forest; in the valley floor, deciduous trees (sugar maples, yellow birch) thrive in a sheltered microclimate.

Hike the Loups Trail (11 km, 447 m elevation gain, difficult): the park's most spectacular viewpoint, with a bird's-eye view down to the Jacques-Cartier Valley and river far below. Shorter alternative: the Éperon Trail (5.4 km, 250 m gain, moderate) also delivers stunning views over the valley and the river at l'Épaule.

activities.text: Canoe or kayak descent on the Jacques-Cartier River: the river alternates between calm stretches and rapids. The park offers boat rentals and a shuttle service for downstream runs (up to 26 km). A unique way to experience the valley from below, framed by forested walls rising on both sides.

to_next_leg.road_type: Route 175 through the Laurentides Wildlife Reserve—dense boreal forest, no services

visits.text: Arrival in Chicoutimi, one of three boroughs that form the City of Saguenay (along with Jonquière and La Baie). La Pulperie de Chicoutimi: built between 1896 and 1930, this former pulp mill was once the world's largest. The complex of brick and stone buildings along the Chicoutimi River has been converted into a regional museum showcasing industrial history, contemporary Québécois art, and the house of Arthur Villeneuve—a barber-turned-painter who transformed his entire home into a work of art by covering every surface—walls, ceiling, floors—with naïve paintings.

La Petite Maison Blanche (The Little White House): this modest wooden home became a symbol of resilience after the July 1996 Saguenay Flood. For 50 hours, torrential rains dumped approximately 270 mm of water on the region, triggering floods and landslides that killed 10 people, forced the evacuation of 16,000, and caused $1.5 billion in damage. Everything around the Little White House was swept away—but it stood firm, alone in the middle of devastated ground. It's now a museum dedicated to the disaster.

Rue Racine, Chicoutimi's main downtown artery: restaurants, cafés, art galleries, and local shops. This is the beating heart of urban Saguenay, with its summer terraces and colorful storefronts. Chicoutimi takes its name from the Innu word 'Ishkutimiu,' meaning 'up to where it is deep'—a reference to the Saguenay River, which becomes navigable at this point.

activities.text: Evening at Saguenay's microbreweries: the region boasts several craft breweries that highlight boreal ingredients—beers brewed with wild blueberries, boreal forest herbs, or local honey.

to_next_leg.road_type: Route 170 along the fjord's south shore

visits.text: La Baie, a Saguenay borough at the head of Ha! Ha! Bay (the name comes from old French 'ha ha,' denoting a dead end or unexpected obstacle—navigators discovered the bay led nowhere). This was once a major timber-shipping port. The historic district preserves buildings from the industrial era and offers views across the bay to the fjord entrance.
Route 170 toward L'Anse-Saint-Jean, hugging the fjord's south shore. The road winds through boreal forest punctuated by glimpses of the fjord's dark waters below.

L'Anse-Saint-Jean: often ranked among Québec's most beautiful villages, this hamlet of about 1,200 is nestled at the head of a cove where the Saint-Jean River flows into the fjord. Its covered bridge, built in 1929, was so iconic it appeared on the old Canadian $1,000 bill. The marina offers spectacular views of the fjord walls rising on either side.

activities.text: Sea kayaking on the fjord from L'Anse-Saint-Jean: paddle at the base of cliffs soaring more than 300 meters above the water, in a fjord that reaches 275 meters deep. Surface water is fresh (it comes from Lac Saint-Jean), while salt water from the Atlantic occupies the depths.

map_keywords: Sentier de la Statue Cap Trinité

visits.text: Baie-Éternité sector of Saguenay Fjord National Park (328 km², established 1983): this is where the fjord reaches its most spectacular dimensions. Two rocky capes face each other across the water: Cap Trinité (518 m) and Cap Éternité (457 m). Their Precambrian rock walls plunge almost vertically into the fjord, which reaches its maximum depth of 275 meters here.

Hike the Statue Trail (7.8 km round-trip, 350 m elevation gain, moderate to difficult): the trail climbs through boreal forest to the summit of Cap Trinité, where the statue of Notre-Dame-du-Saguenay stands. This statue made of pine covered with lead sheets, 7.5 meters tall, was installed in 1881 at the cliff's edge. It was commissioned by Charles-Napoléon Robitaille, a traveling salesman from Chicoutimi who had promised to erect it if he survived nearly drowning in the Saguenay's icy waters. Sculptor Louis Jobin created the work in three blocks of white pine, hollowed out to reduce weight. Hoisting it up the cliff face—cut into 14 pieces, raised 180 meters above the water using pulleys and beams—took eight days.

The Saguenay Fjord stretches approximately 105 km between Saint-Fulgence and Tadoussac. It's the southernmost navigable fjord in North America and one of the world's longest. Its waters have a unique feature: a thin layer of fresh water (7% of the volume) from Lac Saint-Jean floats atop a mass of salt water that entered from the Atlantic via the St. Lawrence River. This stratification creates a unique ecosystem frequented by about 900 belugas that regularly visit the fjord and its mouth (the entire St. Lawrence estuary population numbers about 1,700 individuals).

activities.text: Boat or zodiac cruise on the fjord from Baie-Éternité: navigating at the base of Caps Trinité and Éternité gives you the true measure of these 300-meter cliffs. Your gaze travels upward endlessly. In summer, seals sometimes lounge on rocks at water level.

to_next_leg.road_type: Route 170 then crossing to the fjord's north shore

visits.text: Sainte-Rose-du-Nord, nicknamed 'the pearl of the fjord': this village of about 400 is tucked into a narrow cove, framed by two forested rocky capes. You reach it via a winding road that drops sharply toward the fjord. The small Nature Museum displays collections of rocks, minerals, and natural specimens from the region. The observation wharf offers a direct view of the fjord's dark waters and the cliffs on the opposite shore.

Parc Aventures Cap Jaseux in Saint-Fulgence: an outdoor adventure site perched on the fjord cliffs. The park offers a via ferrata (climbing route on rock face with cables and rungs bolted to the rock), unusual suspended accommodations in the trees (treehouses and glass domes), and sea kayaking on the fjord. The site overlooks the Saguenay directly—the views from the aerial routes are dizzying.

Saint-Fulgence marks the fjord's western end—this is where the Saguenay River ceases to be a fjord and becomes an ordinary river. For experienced hikers, Monts-Valin National Park is accessible 45 minutes north. In winter, its summit is famous for the 'Valley of the Ghosts': frost and snow-covered trees take on spectral shapes.

activities.text: Sea kayaking at sunset on the fjord from Cap Jaseux: paddle between the fjord's rocky walls as the low-angle light paints the cliffs in orange hues. The silence is broken only by the sound of paddles and the cry of gulls.

visits.text: Alma, gateway to the Tour du Lac-Saint-Jean: this city of 30,000 sits where the Grande Décharge River exits the lake to become, farther downstream, the Saguenay River. Île d'Alma, accessible by a pedestrian footbridge, offers a first encounter with the lake's immensity. The Tour du Lac is a marked 263-km circuit—Québec's 19th official tourist route—that makes a complete loop around Lac Saint-Jean.

Pointe-Taillon National Park (92 km², established 1985): a flat peninsula jutting into Lac Saint-Jean, bordered by 15 km of sandy beaches. The park also protects bogs and marshes where numerous birds nest. Lac Saint-Jean covers 1,100 km²—43 km long by 34 km wide. It's a true inland sea fed by about ten rivers that drain an immense boreal forest watershed. In summer, its shallow waters warm enough for swimming.

activities.text: Swimming or cycling on Pointe-Taillon Park trails: the park connects to the Véloroute des Bleuets, a separate 256-km cycling route that circles the lake (not to be confused with the 263-km road Tour du Lac, which follows Route 169 for cars). The fine sand beaches and warm lake water feel more like coastal shores than the interior of the Canadian Shield.

visits.text: Val-Jalbert Historic Village: an authentic ghost town, not a reconstruction. In 1901, Damase Jalbert founded a pulp mill here at the foot of Ouiatchouan Falls, whose hydraulic power ran the machinery. An entire village grew around it: workers' houses, general store, school, convent, hotel. At its peak, about 80 families lived here. When the mill closed in 1927—due to falling pulp prices and competition—everyone left. The buildings remained as they were, frozen in time. Today you walk through the streets of a 1920s village, past houses with empty windows and a ruined mill.

Ouiatchouan Falls, in the heart of Val-Jalbert: 72 meters high—20 meters taller than Niagara Falls. The name comes from the Innu 'Ouiatchouan,' meaning 'rapids with white foam.' A cable car or a 764-step staircase leads to the summit, where the view sweeps across Lac Saint-Jean and the village below. A glass-floor viewing platform lets you stand above the void, right in the heart of the cascade. Upstream, a second waterfall (Maligne Falls, 49 m) is accessible via trail.

Mashteuiatsh (formerly Pointe-Bleue): community of the Pekuakamiulnuatsh First Nation, on Lac Saint-Jean's western shore. It's the region's only Indigenous community. The Ilnu Museum, founded in 1977, traces more than 6,000 years of Pekuakamiulnuatsh history—the Innu people of Lac Saint-Jean. The permanent exhibition 'Tshilanu Ilnuatsh' (We, the Ilnuatsh), winner of a Canadian Museums Association Award of Excellence, explores culture and way of life through the seasons, guided by the voices of Kukum (grandmother) and Mushum (grandfather).

Zoo sauvage de Saint-Félicien (485 hectares): a zoo that reverses the roles. The 'Sentiers de la nature' trail is experienced aboard a small mesh-enclosed train that crosses vast enclosures of caribou, black bears, wolves, bison, and wapiti—visitors are 'caged,' animals roam semi-free in natural habitats. More than 85 species, mainly boreal, coexist in this zoo that presents itself as a living museum of Canadian Shield fauna.

activities.text: Tasting of Lac-Saint-Jean regional products: artisan cheeses (including the famous Perron cheddar, made in Saint-Prime since 1890), tourtière du Lac-Saint-Jean (a slow-cooked meat pie different from tourtière elsewhere in Québec), and local microbrewery beers.

visits.text: Dolbeau-Mistassini, which bills itself as the 'wild blueberry capital of the world.' Every August, the Blueberry Festival celebrates this small fruit emblematic of the region. Blueberry fields—endless expanses where wild lowbush blueberries (Vaccinium angustifolium) grow—cover thousands of hectares around the lake. In season (July-August), you can pick your own in fields open to the public.

Péribonka: this village on Lac Saint-Jean's north shore houses the museum dedicated to Louis Hémon's novel Maria Chapdelaine. Hémon was a Breton writer who arrived in Canada in 1911 and settled in Péribonka in 1912 as a farmhand with the Bédard family. In a few months he wrote a novel about the lives of pioneer settlers clearing land around Lac-Saint-Jean—the cold, the isolation, the attachment to the land. Hémon left Péribonka in June 1913 and died tragically on July 8, struck by a train in Ontario, at age 33. The novel, published posthumously in 1914, was translated into more than 25 languages and became one of the most famous texts about rural Québec.

Parc régional des Grandes-Rivières du lac Saint-Jean: located at the confluence of the Mistassini and Péribonka rivers, two of the most powerful rivers feeding the lake. The park offers hiking trails along the riverbanks, kayaking, and observation points overlooking the rapids. The Péribonka River, made famous by Hémon's novel, flows 451 km from deep in the boreal forest.

activities.text: Wild blueberry picking in the region's blueberry fields (in season, mid-July to mid-August): lowbush blueberry fields stretch as far as the eye can see, covering the ground in a blue-purple carpet. Lac-Saint-Jean blueberries are smaller and more flavorful than cultivated blueberries—they're used to make pies, jams, wines, and local liqueurs.

to_next_leg.road_type: Route 155 South—Saint-Maurice valley through boreal forest

visits.text: OPTION A - Return via Route 155 (Saint-Maurice Valley): this spectacular 230-km road follows the Saint-Maurice River through nearly pristine boreal forest. The Saint-Maurice River, once the highway for log drivers floating timber down to mills in Trois-Rivières, flows 563 km from the Gouin Reservoir. The road follows its meanders, rapids, and lake-like widenings. The route's most striking moment: when you leave Lac Saint-Jean from Chambord and the immense expanse of water appears suddenly, 'like the sea.'

Stop in La Tuque, a small forestry town on the Saint-Maurice—a rest and refueling point midway. The town was an important log-driving center and remains shaped by the forest industry. Félix-Antoine Savard, author of the novel Menaud, Master Logger (1937) celebrating Saint-Maurice log drivers, drew on this region for his work.

OPTION B - Return via Chicoutimi → Tadoussac (Route 172 East): this alternative follows the Saguenay Fjord's north shore to its mouth. Stop at the Sainte-Marguerite River, where belugas regularly visit the shallow cove waters (observable from a platform on shore). Arrival in Tadoussac, at the confluence of the Saguenay and St. Lawrence, is one of Québec's most beautiful landscapes. This option is recommended if you haven't done the Eastern Canada road trip and want to discover Tadoussac.

activities.text: If option A: stop at La Mauricie National Park (536 km², midway between La Tuque and Trois-Rivières) if time allows—maple forest, lakes, and waterfalls in the Laurentian hills.

visits.text: If option A: Route 155 toward Trois-Rivières, then Highway 40 East toward Québec. The final stretch crosses the St. Lawrence plain, with its farmland and riverside villages. Arrival in Québec City by early afternoon.

If option B: Tadoussac → Québec via Route 138 West through Charlevoix (about 3 hours). The road hugs the St. Lawrence River, passing through Baie-Saint-Paul and the rolling landscapes of Charlevoix—a region designated a UNESCO Biosphere Reserve. Arrival in Québec City late afternoon.

activities.text: Final stroll through Old Québec to close the loop: the 1,200-km circuit through boreal forest, fjord, and lake ends where it began, at the foot of the ramparts of North America's only fortified city.

seo.intro_paragraph: From Québec City to the cliffs of the Saguenay Fjord and around Lac Saint-Jean: 1,200 km in a loop through boreal forest, villages clinging to the walls of a 105-km fjord (the southernmost navigable fjord in North America), an inland sea of 1,100 km², the ancient culture of the Pekuakamiulnuatsh, the ghost village of Val-Jalbert, and the boreal terroir—wild blueberries, artisan cheeses, microbreweries, and Lac-Saint-Jean tourtières.
seo.seo_hook: Cliffs plunging 300 meters into a fjord where belugas swim, a ghost town frozen in the 1920s at the foot of a 72-meter waterfall, an inland sea ringed by sandy beaches and endless blueberry fields—the Saguenay-Lac-Saint-Jean is wild Québec, three hours from the city.

itineraries.title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies
itineraries.subtitle: From New France to badlands dinosaurs, 5,000 km through boreal forest, Great Lakes, and prairies to the Rocky Mountain peaks
itineraries.notes: Days 7 and 8: two consecutive long driving days (480 km each) through northern Ontario. Plan for early morning departures.

visits.text: Place Royale, birthplace of French America: this is where Champlain built his fortified habitation in 1608 with 28 men—20 of whom would die of scurvy during that first brutal winter. In 1686, Intendant Champigny installed a bust of Louis XIV here, giving the square its name. The outline of Champlain's second habitation (demolished in 1624, rebuilt in stone between 1626 and 1627) is traced in the pavement near the church.

Step inside the Notre-Dame-des-Victoires church, built in 1688 directly on the foundations of Champlain's original habitation. It's the oldest stone church in Canada with its original walls still standing. Severely damaged during the 1759 siege, it was hastily rebuilt, then in 1816 architect François Baillairgé undertook major renovations that gave the church its current appearance.

Wander down Rue du Petit-Champlain, one of the oldest commercial streets in North America. The Breakneck Stairs (Escalier Casse-Cou), built as early as the 17th century, connect the Lower Town to the summit of Cap Diamant. The funicular, built beside the former residence of the Mississippi's European discoverer, offers sweeping views over the St. Lawrence River.

The Château Frontenac has dominated Cap Diamant since 1893, when the Canadian Pacific Railway inaugurated this grand château-style hotel designed by architect Bruce Price. Inspired by 14th and 15th-century Loire Valley castles, it originally had 170 rooms—now over 600. In 1943, Churchill and Roosevelt met here for the Quebec Conference. The Dufferin Terrace, right in front, offers panoramic views of the river and Île d'Orléans.

Walk the 4.6 km of fortifications that encircle Old Québec. Québec is the only walled city north of Mexico—a distinction recognized by UNESCO, which inscribed the historic district as a World Heritage Site in 1985. The Saint-Jean, Saint-Louis, and Kent gates served as strategic entry points. In the 1870s, Governor Lord Dufferin saved these walls from demolition.

activities.text: Evening stroll through the Petit-Champlain quarter: artisan boutiques, galleries, and restaurants housed in 18th-century buildings.

visits.text: Notre-Dame Basilica on Place d'Armes: constructed between 1824 and 1829 to designs by New York architect James O'Donnell, this was Canada's first major Gothic Revival church. It could hold up to 10,000 worshippers. The blue vaulted ceiling studded with golden stars and the massive carved altarpiece by Henri Bouriché date from the 1872-1880 renovations by Victor Bourgeau. The Jean-Baptiste bourdon bell in the west tower weighs 10,900 kg.

Walk through Old Montréal to Pointe-à-Callière, the exact spot where Montréal was founded in 1642. The archaeology museum, built over the remains of the first Fort Ville-Marie, displays 17th-century foundations. Across the way, Place Royale de Montréal—named in 1892 for the city's 250th anniversary—served as a marketplace as early as 1676.

Stroll along the Old Port to Bonsecours Market, built in 1847 in neoclassical style with its silver dome. For a time it served as the Parliament of the United Province of Canada. The adjacent Notre-Dame-de-Bon-Secours Chapel dates from 1771 and contains sailors' ex-votos hanging from the ceiling.

activities.text: Dinner in Old Montréal: the cobblestone lanes between Rue Saint-Paul and Rue de la Commune are packed with restaurants set in former 19th-century grey stone warehouses.

visits.text: Climb to the summit of Mount Royal (233 m), a 200-hectare park designed in 1876 by Frederick Law Olmsted, the same landscape architect who created New York's Central Park. The Kondiaronk Belvedere—named after the Huron-Wendat chief who signed the Great Peace of Montréal in 1701—offers unobstructed views of downtown, the river, and the Montérégie hills.

Descend toward the Plateau Mont-Royal, a neighborhood recognizable by its wrought-iron outdoor staircases and colorful facades. These stairs, typical of 19th-century Montréal architecture, saved precious interior space in the triplex buildings. Avenue Mont-Royal is lined with local shops and cafés.

Continue to Mile End, a former working-class neighborhood turned creative heart of Montréal. This is where you'll find the two rival bagel bakeries, wood-firing according to traditional methods inherited from Eastern European Jewish immigrants who arrived in the early 20th century. Montréal bagels—smaller, denser, and boiled in honey water—bear no resemblance to their New York cousins.

activities.text: Wood-fired bagel tasting in Mile End, followed by a walk through streets lined with street art and murals.

map_keywords: Musée des beaux-arts du Canada Ottawa

visits.text: Parliament Hill overlooks the Ottawa River from a limestone outcrop. The Gothic Revival buildings, constructed between 1859 and 1866, were ravaged by fire in February 1916—only the Library, saved by an employee who closed its iron doors, survived. The Peace Tower, completed in 1927, rises 92 meters. Its carillon of 53 bells, commissioned to commemorate the 1918 armistice, still rings daily over the capital.

The Rideau Canal stretches 202 km from Ottawa to Lake Ontario. Built between 1826 and 1832 under the direction of Colonel John By, it mobilized up to 5,000 workers annually—mainly Irish immigrants and French Canadians—hundreds of whom died from malaria or accidents. Designed as a military route after the War of 1812, it became a commercial waterway and later a natural skating rink in winter. A UNESCO site since 2007, it's the only World Heritage Site in Ontario.

ByWard Market, operating since 1826, bears Colonel By's name. It's one of the oldest and largest public markets in Canada. Stalls of local products—cheeses, maple syrup, pastries—sit alongside art galleries and restaurants in 19th-century stone and brick buildings.

Cross the bridge over the canal to the National Gallery of Canada, recognizable by its granite and glass towers. It houses the world's largest collection of Inuit art—stone, bone, and ivory sculptures from communities across the Far North. The building, designed by architect Moshe Safdie and opened in 1988, plays with natural light through glass canopies that frame views of Parliament Hill.

activities.text: Bike ride or walk along the Rideau Canal in late afternoon: the path follows the Ottawa Locks, the eight-step flight of locks connecting the canal to the Ottawa River.

visits.text: North Bay, "Gateway to the North," developed with the arrival of the Canadian Pacific Railway in 1882. The restored historic station now houses a museum tracing the region's railway history. The city also owes its fame to the Dionne quintuplets, born here in 1934—an event that captured worldwide attention.

Lake Nipissing, Ontario's third-largest lake entirely within the province (875 km², 65 km long), was a major trade route for the Anishinaabe peoples. Its Ojibwe name paradoxically means "little body of water"—compared to the nearby Great Lakes. In 1610, explorer Étienne Brûlé became the first European to navigate these waters, along the fur trade route linking the Ottawa River to Lake Huron via the French River.

activities.text: Sunset walk along North Bay's waterfront: the white sand beaches of Shabogesic Beach rank among Ontario's finest, facing the lake's shallow waters (average depth only 4.5 m).

map_keywords: Sault Ste. Marie Canal National Historic Site
Bushplane Heritage Centre Sault Ste. Marie

visits.text: Sault Ste. Marie Canal National Historic Site: completed in 1895, this canal was the world's longest lock and the first to operate on electricity. It's the final link in the all-Canadian navigation route connecting the Atlantic to Lake Superior. The rapids of the St. Marys River—called Baawaating ("place of the rapids") in Anishinaabemowin—served as a fishing and trading center for Indigenous peoples for millennia. The historic red sandstone buildings were cut from stone excavated during canal construction.

Stroll the boardwalk along the St. Marys River: from this promenade, you can watch freighters transit between Lake Huron and Lake Superior via the American locks (four US locks, still in commercial operation). The 6-meter elevation difference between the two lakes creates the rapids that give the city its name—"sault" meaning "rapid" in old French.

activities.text: Scenic drive from North Bay via Sudbury and the Trans-Canada Highway (Route 17), a full day's drive through the boreal forest of the Canadian Shield.

map_keywords: Lake Superior Provincial Park Agawa Bay

visits.text: The Agawa Rock pictographs in Lake Superior Provincial Park (1,550 km², established 1944): on a white crystalline granite cliff rising 15 stories high, 35 red ochre paintings created by Ojibwe spiritual leaders between the 17th and 18th centuries. The most famous depicts Mishipeshu, the Great Lynx of the water, guardian of copper. The access trail (800 m) descends between rock crevices to a ledge on the lake's edge—accessible only in calm weather.

Stop at Old Woman Bay, a cove framed by sheer cliffs plunging directly into Lake Superior. This region of Algoma is where the Group of Seven—those 1920s Canadian painters—created some of their most celebrated canvases. The landscape of twisted pines, pink rock, and deep blue water resembles a living painting.

activities.text: Hike the Nokomis Trail or the coastal trail in Lake Superior Provincial Park, through boreal forest on the Canadian Shield.

map_keywords: Sleeping Giant Provincial Park Thunder Bay
Fort William Historical Park Thunder Bay

visits.text: From Hillcrest Park or the Terry Fox Memorial, observe the Sleeping Giant—a formation of diabase mesas on the Sibley Peninsula that traces the silhouette of a giant lying on his back. According to Ojibwe legend, it's Nanabijou, spirit of the deep waters, turned to stone for revealing the location of a silver mine to Europeans. The cliffs rank among Ontario's highest (250 m).

The Terry Fox Memorial on the Trans-Canada Highway east of Thunder Bay marks where the young runner had to stop his Marathon of Hope on September 1, 1980, after 143 days and 5,373 km running on an artificial leg. The bronze statue, facing west—the direction he never reached—overlooks Lake Superior.

Fort William Historical Park recreates the continent's largest inland fur trading post as it was in 1816. Forty-two buildings bring to life the North West Company depot, where voyageurs, traders, and First Nations gathered each summer to exchange furs, provisions, and news.

activities.text: Hike to the top of the Sleeping Giant (Top of the Giant Trail): 22 km round trip, panoramic views of Lake Superior from the cliffs of the Sibley Peninsula.

visits.text: Kenora, Ontario's last city before Manitoba, sits on the shores of Lake of the Woods—a sprawling lake covering 4,350 km² dotted with over 14,500 islands. A long day's drive through boreal forest, with stops at lookouts along the Trans-Canada Highway. The region was a crossroads of fur trade routes between the Great Lakes and the Prairies.
Wander the lakefront in downtown Kenora and spot Husky the Muskie, a 12-meter-tall giant muskellunge statue—a nod to the sport fishing that made Lake of the Woods famous.

activities.text: Sunset on Lake of the Woods: the calm waters mirror the pines and rocks of the Canadian Shield, a landscape typical of northwestern Ontario.

map_keywords: Canadian Museum for Human Rights Winnipeg

visits.text: The Forks, at the confluence of the Red and Assiniboine rivers: a gathering place, trading center, and site of peace treaties for Indigenous peoples for over 6,000 years. Archaeological excavations have uncovered nearly 400,000 artifacts, some dating back to 1100 CE. Today, the indoor market and riverside docks animate this national historic site.

The Canadian Museum for Human Rights, adjacent to The Forks: opened in 2014, designed by architect Antoine Predock, it's the first national museum built outside the federal capital and the world's first dedicated to human rights. Its 11 galleries are connected by alabaster ramps. The cornerstone comes from Runnymede, England, where the Magna Carta was sealed in 1215.

The Exchange District, north of Portage Avenue: some twenty blocks of brick and stone buildings constructed between 1880 and 1920, forming one of North America's best-preserved assemblages of early 20th-century commercial architecture. These streets witnessed the Winnipeg General Strike of 1919—six weeks of total paralysis, 30,000 strikers, and a Mounted Police charge on June 21 ("Bloody Saturday") that left two dead and marked the history of the Canadian labor movement.

Cross the Provencher Bridge to Saint-Boniface, Western Canada's oldest francophone neighborhood. Saint-Boniface Cathedral, whose 1908 stone facade survived the 1968 fire, stands in ruins framing the new church. This is where Louis Riel, Métis leader and father of Manitoba, is buried in the parish cemetery.

activities.text: Evening at The Forks Market: stalls of Indigenous, Métis, and multicultural cuisine in one of the Prairies' liveliest markets.

visits.text: Riding Mountain National Park (2,969 km², officially established 1933) stands atop the Manitoba Escarpment, 400 meters above the surrounding prairies. It's Canada's only national park protecting three ecosystems converging in one place: grasslands, boreal forest, and eastern deciduous forest. The East Gate, a national historic site, is a monumental log-and-stone entrance built in the 1930s by 1,200 workers as part of Great Depression relief programs.

Walk along Clear Lake from the village of Wasagaming: this crystalline lake, 34 meters deep, is the recreational heart of the park. The village retains the charm of a 1930s resort town, with its log buildings and period theatre.

The Lake Audy bison enclosure shelters a herd of about forty plains bison in 500 hectares of native prairie. These bison recall the immense herds that once covered the Canadian Prairies before their near-extinction in the 19th century.

activities.text: Hike the Grey Owl Trail (8.9 km one way) to Grey Owl's cabin, built in 1931 beside Beaver Lodge Lake. Archibald Belaney, a Briton who reinvented himself under the Indigenous identity of Grey Owl, was one of Canada's first conservationists. Hired by Parks Canada to reintroduce beavers, he lived six months in this L-shaped log cabin with his two beavers, Jelly Roll and Rawhide.

map_keywords: Royal Canadian Mounted Police Heritage Centre Regina

visits.text: Stop in Moose Jaw, halfway between Winnipeg and Regina: beneath downtown streets, a network of underground tunnels served as refuge for Chinese immigrants persecuted by early 20th-century exclusion laws. These passages, discovered in the 1980s, are now recreated as museum tours.

Wascana Centre in the heart of Regina spreads across 930 hectares around an artificial lake—one of Canada's largest urban parks. The Saskatchewan Legislative Building, constructed between 1908 and 1912 in Beaux-Arts style, reflects in the calm waters of Wascana Lake. On its lawns in 1935, the tragic end of the On-to-Ottawa Trek unfolded when thousands of unemployed men heading to the federal capital were stopped by police in a bloody riot.

The RCMP Heritage Centre traces the history of the Royal Canadian Mounted Police since its creation in 1873. Regina is the RCMP's only training center in Canada: all cadets pass through here. The museum displays uniforms, equipment, and accounts of the first mounted patrols across the Western Prairies.

activities.text: Long driving day across the Manitoba and Saskatchewan prairies (490 km): straight roads to the horizon, canola and wheat fields, grain elevators punctuating the skyline.

visits.text: The Royal Tyrrell Museum, opened in 1985 in Midland Provincial Park, is Canada's only museum dedicated entirely to paleontology. Named for Joseph Tyrrell, a geologist who discovered the first dinosaur skull in the Red Deer River valley in 1884 while searching for coal. The collection exceeds 160,000 fossil specimens, with over 800 on permanent display. The Borealopelta, an armored nodosaur discovered in the oil sands in 2011, is one of the world's best-preserved fossils—skin and stomach contents included.

Horseshoe Canyon, 17 km west of Drumheller: a spectacular breach carved into the flat prairie, revealing layers of sandstone, mudstone, and coal 70 million years old. The horizontal strata, alternating grey, beige, and ochre, tell the passage from a subtropical climate to the ice age. A short but steep trail descends to the canyon floor.

The Drumheller Hoodoos: these sandstone columns capped with harder blocks result from millions of years of erosion by wind and water. Each pillar stands balanced thanks to its "cap" of hard rock protecting the soft sandstone below. Further along, the Rosedale Suspension Bridge, a wire pedestrian walkway built for coal miners, sways above the valley.

activities.text: Scenic drive on the Dinosaur Trail (48 km loop around Drumheller): cross the Red Deer River on the Bleriot Ferry, one of Alberta's last cable ferries, in service since 1913.

visits.text: Banff's story begins in 1883, when three Canadian Pacific Railway workers accidentally discovered sulfurous hot springs on the flank of Sulphur Mountain. In 1885, the federal government created a 26 km² reserve around these springs—the seed of what would become Canada's first national park and the third in the world. The alpine village of Banff then stretched along Banff Avenue, backed by Cascade Mountain (2,998 m).

Take the Banff Gondola to the summit of Sulphur Mountain at 2,281 m: the panorama encompasses the Bow Valley, Lake Minnewanka, and dozens of Rocky Mountain peaks. At the summit, a boardwalk leads to a viewpoint and the Sanson Peak weather station, manned by a solitary observer for 30 years in the early 20th century.

Descend to Bow Falls, where the Bow River cascades over limestone benches. The Fairmont Banff Springs, visible from the falls, was built in 1888 in the same château style as Québec's Frontenac—both commissioned by the Canadian Pacific to attract wealthy tourists to its trains.

activities.text: Soak in the Banff Upper Hot Springs: outdoor thermal spring at 47°C, at 1,585 m elevation, with views of Mount Rundle. Indigenous peoples frequented these springs long before European arrival.

visits.text: Lake Louise, at 1,731 m elevation, owes its turquoise color to glacial flour—rock particles finely ground by glaciers that refract light. The lake was named in 1884 in honor of Princess Louise Caroline Alberta, fourth daughter of Queen Victoria (the province also bears her name). The Plain of Six Glaciers trail (13.8 km round trip) leads to a teahouse built in 1924 by Swiss guides of the Canadian Pacific.

Moraine Lake in the Valley of the Ten Peaks is accessible from June to October only. This landscape once graced the back of old Canadian twenty-dollar bills. The ten peaks surrounding it rise between 3,000 and 3,500 m.

activities.text: Hike Johnston Canyon (5.4 km round trip): a developed trail with catwalks and metal bridges along a limestone gorge leads to the lower falls (30 m) then the upper falls. The canyon walls, sculpted by erosion, reveal limestone layers 350 million years old.

visits.text: The Icefields Parkway (230 km between Lake Louise and Jasper) cuts through the heart of the Canadian Rockies. Stop at Peyto Lake, whose shape evokes a wolf's head as seen from Bow Summit viewpoint at 2,069 m—the highway's highest point. The lake's brilliant blue color comes from glacial flour fed by Peyto Glacier.

The Columbia Icefield covers 325 km² and feeds glaciers that drain toward three different oceans—Atlantic, Pacific, and Arctic. The Athabasca Glacier, the most accessible, extends 6 km from the icefield. Its thickness reaches 365 m in places, but it recedes about 5 meters per year.

Athabasca Falls in Jasper National Park: the Athabasca River plunges 23 m into a narrow gorge carved in quartzite, a rock so hard it forces the water to compress into a passage less than 18 meters wide. The power of the flow is particularly impressive in early summer during snowmelt.

Maligne Canyon, 11 km from Jasper townsite: the deepest gorge in the Canadian Rockies (50 m), carved by the Maligne River through limestone. Six bridges allow you to peer into the canyon depths. In winter, the gorge freezes into a landscape of ice cascades.

activities.text: Hike the Valley of the Five Lakes (4.6 km, easy) in Jasper National Park: five small lakes in shades ranging from turquoise to jade, fed by underground springs.

seo.h1_title: Cross-Canada Journey: From Québec City to the Rockies
seo.intro_paragraph: From Québec City's Place Royale, founded in 1608, to the glaciers of Jasper: 5,000 km of road through Old Montréal, the capital Ottawa, the boreal forests of the Canadian Shield, the wild shores of Lake Superior, the infinite prairies of Manitoba and Saskatchewan, the badlands of Drumheller, and the Rocky Mountain peaks at Banff and Jasper.
seo.seo_hook: The Canadian Shield unfolds its thousands of lakes, the prairies roll to the horizon, the badlands reveal their 70-million-year-old fossils, and the Rockies rise at the end of the road. Crossing Canada is a journey on a continental scale.
featured_list: Québec City: walled birthplace of French America
Ottawa: capital on the UNESCO Rideau Canal
Lake Superior: Ojibwe pictographs and wild coastlines
Thunder Bay: the Sleeping Giant of Ojibwe legend
Winnipeg: Museum for Human Rights at The Forks
Drumheller: badlands and dinosaurs at the Royal Tyrrell Museum
Banff and Jasper: turquoise lakes and Rocky Mountain glaciers
